The segment opens with an aerial view of a campus featuring a clock tower and lush trees, accompanied by a subtle instrumental background score. A male voice, later identified as Tom Marcukic, reflects on being drawn in by the sight and sound of something in Ohio. The visuals transition to still images: a childhood photo of a young boy, a woman in a graduation cap and gown, and a man in a centrifuge test. A female narrator then details Tom Marcukic's academic journey, earning a PhD in advanced space propulsion from Princeton, followed by five years working for the US government with the Air Force and NASA. The scene shifts to a close-up of Mars, as Tom Marcukic, now seen as a man in a black Firefly-branded polo shirt in a workshop with rocket components, explains that current travel to Mars takes six to eight months, but NASA technology could reduce it to weeks. He discusses the challenge of getting cool technology into space efficiently and mentions Elon Musk and other companies developing new space ideas to lower costs and increase access. The video then shows a SpaceX rocket launching with a massive plume of fire and smoke, followed by Tom Marcukic speaking again, and a still image of him beside a rocket engine. The female narrator explains he became SpaceX's first Director of Test Operations in McGregor, Texas. Tom Marcukic describes building the facility, testing rocket engines day and night, including Falcon 1s and Falcon 9s, and becoming intimately knowledgeable about launch vehicles, as intense flames erupt from a rocket engine test. An aerial view of a rocket launch site with a large white rocket appears. The segment concludes with a still image of a man in a red polo shirt in front of a rocket nose cone, and a Blue Origin New Shepard rocket on a launch pad, as the narrator states Marcukic went on to work at Blue Origin.

event_blocks.summary: Ground crew in red jumpsuits prepared a Virgin Galactic carrier aircraft on a tarmac at night, which then flew during the day carrying a spaceplane, followed by a satellite deployment in space with solar panels unfurling. A female narrator mentioned Virgin Galactic and a person's past role as VP of propulsion, leading development of rocket engine systems and new launch vehicles like LauncherOne. The scene shifted to a satellite in orbit above Earth with a swirling storm below, then to engineers in white suits testing a gold-wrapped satellite in a cleanroom. Tom Markusic began speaking about satellites shrinking in size and increasing in capabilities, and a divergence between new space companies and customer needs for this new class of satellites. Tom Markusic, wearing a black Firefly-branded polo shirt, was then seen seated in a workshop with large rocket components, revealing he decided to start his own transformative space transportation company, which was the genesis of Firefly. The female narrator interjected that in January 2014, Markusic founded Firefly Space Systems with PJ King and Michael Bloom. Tom Markusic's voice returned, explaining they started with the notion of building the world's simplest and most economical launch vehicle, but funding fell through, forcing the company to declare bankruptcy in 2017. As close-ups of a Firefly rocket engine with its carbon fiber nozzle and yellow gear were shown, followed by engineers working on computers in a construction site-like office with a 3D rocket engine model, Tom Markusic reappeared standing beside a large rocket engine, gesturing, and explained the rapid burn rates in the business, noting Firefly's were on the order of $10 million a month. The segment concluded with two men, one in a Firefly hoodie, inspecting a large rocket engine in a workshop, with the CNBC logo visible throughout.

raw_segments: The video opens with ground crew in red jumpsuits preparing a Virgin Galactic carrier aircraft on a tarmac at night, transitioning to the aircraft in flight during the day, carrying a spaceplane. A satellite deployment in space follows, with solar panels unfurling against the blackness. A female narrator mentions Virgin Galactic, noting a person's past role as VP of propulsion, leading development of rocket engine systems and new launch vehicles like LauncherOne. The scene shifts to a satellite in orbit above Earth with a swirling storm below, then to engineers in white suits testing a gold-wrapped satellite in a cleanroom. A male voice, later identified as Tom Markusic, begins speaking about "satellites, which had in the past been these giant monolithic pieces of hardware, were shrinking in size and increasing in capabilities," and a "divergence between what the up and coming new space companies were doing and what the customers needed for this new class of satellites." The man in a black Firefly-branded polo shirt, Tom Markusic, is then seen seated in a workshop with large rocket components, continuing to speak, revealing he "ultimately decided that if I really wanted to create a transformative space transportation company, I needed to step out of my own and start my own company and that was the genesis of Firefly." The female narrator interjects, stating that "In January of 2014, Markusic founded Firefly Space Systems, alongside PJ King and Michael Bloom." Tom Markusic's voice returns, explaining they "started with the notion that we would build the world's simplest launch vehicle, the most economical launch vehicle we could." The narrator then adds that "funding fell through, forcing the company to declare bankruptcy in 2017." As close-ups of a Firefly rocket engine with its carbon fiber nozzle and yellow gear are shown, followed by engineers working on computers in a construction site-like office with a 3D rocket engine model, and Tom Markusic reappears standing beside a large rocket engine, gesturing, he explains the rapid "burn rates" in the business, noting Firefly's were "on the order of $10 million a month." The segment concludes with two men, one in a Firefly hoodie, inspecting a large rocket engine in a workshop. The CNBC logo remains in the top right corner throughout.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative traces the resurrection and development of Firefly Aerospace, beginning with Tom Markusic reflecting on past financial failures and shutdowns, followed by the securing of new funding from Newsphere Ventures, led by Ukrainian investor Max Polyakov, which effectively resurrected the company. Markusic, now reinstated as founder and CEO, confirmed the company became Firefly Aerospace, funded by Newsphere for approximately $200 million, their entire seed round, to develop viable technology for flight. Engineers are depicted monitoring rocket data in a control room, and Firefly later raised an additional $175 million. The company's 'Alpha' rocket, described as the world's largest all-carbon fiber composite rocket with a patented, simple engine, is highlighted, capable of carrying up to 1,000 kilograms to lower orbit for about $15 million per launch, with future upgrades planned. Shea Ferring, SVP of Spacecraft, details the four-year development timeline, emphasizing in-house manufacturing for cost and schedule control. Intense rocket engine tests, particularly of the 'Reaver' first-stage engine, are showcased at a test site in Briggs, Texas, involving acceptance testing protocols to tune the engine and ensure workmanship. Meagan Roth, Sr. Manager Launch and Test Operations, explains the testing process on the TSQ stand, which accommodates both first and second stages. The segment culminates with dramatic footage of a rocket launch, igniting with a massive plume of fire and smoke as it lifts off from the pad, signifying the company's progress towards spaceflight.

CNBC Logo.description: A white CNBC logo with a peacock design, consistently visible in the top right corner throughout the segment.

Man in Suit.description: This is Tom Marcuzic, speaking in a bright living room, reflecting on the past failure of his space ventures ("ran out of money, we had to shut down") and the subsequent securing of new funding to revive his ambitions.

Firefly Rocket.description: A large rocket with a black and white body featuring a stylized 'A' logo, shown during assembly and on the launch pad, with text overlays identifying it as 'Alpha', 'Blue Ghost', and 'Future in space'.

Man in Black Jacket.description: This is likely Tom Marcuzic, seen inspecting the engines at the base of a rocket on a launch pad, visually representing the hands-on involvement in the Firefly Aerospace venture he discusses.

Engineers in Office.description: A group of men in casual clothing, including t-shirts and caps, working on computers in a partially constructed office space with exposed scaffolding and a large screen displaying a 3D rocket engine model.

Man in Black Hoodie.description: A man wearing a black hoodie, walking near the rocket's base during inspection.

Rocket.description: Initially seen as a black and white 'NOOSPHERE' branded rocket on a launch pad, it is later shown as the 'FIREFLY' branded 'Alpha' rocket during assembly in a hangar, featuring white lettering and yellow accents, with text overlays identifying it as 'Alpha', 'Blue Ghost', and 'Future in space'. The audio reveals it is the product of Firefly Aerospace, funded by Newsphere Ventures with a $200 million seed round and an additional $175 million, representing "viable technology to go out and fly."

Rocket Engine Test.description: A close-up of a rocket engine test at a SpaceX facility, with intense flames and smoke erupting from the engine, accompanied by a loud, powerful roar and driving electronic music.

Tom Markusic.description: Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, seen seated in a workshop wearing a black Firefly-branded polo shirt with large rocket components behind him, he speaks about the Firefly Alpha rocket's design as a 'two-stage expendable vehicle,' its 'all carbon fiber composite' construction, 'patented, world's simplest rocket engine,' its capacity of 'up to 1,000 kilograms' (eventually '1,500 kilograms') to lower orbit, and its 'about $15 million per launch' price, also noting the 'about $100 million' investment typically required for a launch company.

Rocket Launch Footage.description: Dynamic footage of a rocket engine test with bright flames and smoke, followed by archival footage of the Apollo 11 launch on July 16, 1969, shown in a yellow-bordered frame against a starry background.

Michael Sheetz.description: A man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit living room with large windows, a white sofa, and a lamp.

Shea Ferring.description: SVP of Spacecraft at Firefly Aerospace, seen wearing a black Firefly-branded shirt and speaking in a workshop with a golden spacecraft in the background, he explains that Firefly 'build[s] everything ourselves' to control cost and schedule, detailing a four-year development timeline including 'a year of design,' 'two years of developmental testing,' and 'one year of qualifying hardware,' further emphasizing the need for 'super high propulsion, super light structures' that are 'very, very efficient' but 'hard to test'.

Woman in Workshop.description: A woman with curly hair, wearing a dark zip-up top, speaking in a clear, informative female voice in a workshop with a large rocket section in the background, detailing the diverse engineering disciplines required at Firefly.

Rocket Engine.description: A large, complex rocket engine, identified by Meagan Roth as the 'Reaver engine' and Firefly's first-stage engine, with visible piping, valves, and a metallic nozzle, shown during multiple test firings with intense flames and smoke, producing a powerful rumbling roar, mounted on a test stand in an open field and inside a test chamber. Its purpose is to be installed on the vehicle, and it undergoes an 'acceptance testing protocol' (ATP) to tune it and ensure workmanship and quality.

Meagan Roth.description: Sr. Manager Launch and Test Operations at Firefly Aerospace, wearing a black Firefly-branded polo shirt with a white logo and sunglasses, speaking to the camera beside a rocket engine and later on an observation deck, with a clear, authoritative voice, explaining the purpose and testing of the 'Reaver engine' and the 'TSQ' test stand.

Test Facility.description: An industrial test site with metal structures, support towers, and a concrete pad, identified by Meagan Roth as 'TSQ' (Test Stand Quad), located in a flat, grassy landscape under a clear sky, featuring a large engine test stand for testing rocket stages one and two, and an observation deck. It is where the engine's fire goes through during ignition.

Inside a well-lit living room, Marcuzic, in a dark suit, speaks directly to the camera. A male narrator details how the company was "effectively resurrected by an investment firm called Newsphere Ventures," funded and led by Ukrainian investor Max Polyakov. Aerial shots reveal the full scale of the launch complex with a yellow crane lifting a rocket section onto the launch tower. In a workshop, Marcuzic, now in a black Firefly-branded polo shirt, speaks to the camera with large rocket components behind him, confirming "It became Firefly Aerospace. Tom Marcuzic was reinstated as founder, CEO." He further explains that "Newsphere funded Firefly for about $200 million," which was their "whole seed round" to achieve "viable technology to go out and fly." The segment concludes with close-ups of the rocket’s black body, marked with white 'FIRELY' lettering and yellow accents, being assembled in a hangar, with text overlays identifying it as 'Alpha', 'Blue Ghost', and 'Future in space'. Marcuzic's voice returns, reflecting on entrepreneurs and asserting that "This new company has a better business plan, a better technology plan, a better funding plan than the other company without a doubt," as light, hopeful background music begins to build, and the female narrator concludes, "To get to space..." The segment opens with Michael Sheetz, a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit living room with large windows, a white sofa, and a lamp, as a narrator states that Firefly has spent the past few years building its Alpha launch vehicle. The scene transitions to a CGI animation of a rocket in orbit above Earth, followed by a close-up of a rocket engine test at a SpaceX facility, showing intense flames and smoke erupting from its metallic structure. A rocket launch from a grassy pad then produces a massive plume of fire and smoke under a twilight sky, with the SpaceX logo visible. Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, appears in a black Firefly-branded polo shirt, seated in a workshop with large rocket components behind him, explaining that 'Alpha is Firefly's two-stage expendable vehicle,' the 'world's largest all carbon fiber composite rocket' with a 'patented, world's simplest rocket engine.' He continues, detailing its capacity to carry 'up to 1,000 kilograms' to lower orbit, priced at 'about $15 million per launch,' with future upgrades allowing 'about 1,500 kilograms.' As light, rhythmic, slightly futuristic background music begins, the video shows a computer screen displaying a 3D model of a rocket engine, a control room with engineers monitoring multiple screens, and another rocket launch with a bright flame and smoke. A close-up then shows a rocket with a stylized 'A' logo being assembled. Shea Ferring, SVP of Spacecraft at Firefly Aerospace, in a black Firefly-branded shirt, speaks from a workshop with a golden spacecraft in the background, stating they 'build everything ourselves' to control cost and schedule, outlining a four-year development timeline from 'a year of design' to 'two years of developmental testing' and 'one year of qualifying hardware.' Tom Markusic then returns, discussing the 'about $100 million' investment typically expected before successfully reaching orbit, followed by Shea Ferring emphasizing the need for 'super high propulsion, super light structures' that are 'very, very efficient' but 'hard to test.' The segment concludes with visuals of a rocket engine test with intense flames and smoke, and a rocket launch with a bright flame and smoke under a clear sky, all while the CNBC logo remains consistently visible in the top right corner.

The video opens with a dynamic office environment at Firefly Aerospace, where engineers in black Firefly-branded shirts and masks are intensely focused on computer screens displaying technical data and 3D rocket models, emphasizing a collaborative, high-tech atmosphere. The scene transitions to a workshop where Tom Markusic, a man in a black Firefly polo shirt, sits on a stool in front of a large rocket component, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, authoritative male voice about the challenges of building hardware and a team, noting Firefly has doubled its team size annually to about 500 people. He emphasizes the need for "really smart engineers." A woman with curly hair, also in a workshop with a large rocket section, adds in a clear, informative female voice that many Firefly employees come from other space companies like SpaceX, Blue Origin, and NASA, before listing the numerous specialized engineering disciplines required, from structures and mechanical to avionics, software, and trajectory. Tom Markusic then reiterates the importance of testing, stating that despite investment in time and people, "you don't really know if the thing's going to work until you go out and test it." The scene shifts to an outdoor test site in Briggs, Texas, under a blue sky, where an intense rocket engine test unfolds in dramatic close-up, with powerful flames and smoke erupting from the engine, accompanied by a loud, powerful roar and driving electronic music, highlighting the progression from design to real-world validation. A white CNBC logo with a peacock design is consistently visible in the top right corner throughout the segment.

The segment opens with intense close-up shots of a rocket engine test, showcasing bright white and green flames erupting from the nozzle, accompanied by an intense, rumbling engine roar and dramatic, driving background music. A wide shot of the test site reveals industrial structures and billowing smoke under a clear sky. The camera then captures a side view of the engine firing with a massive plume of fire and smoke, transitioning to a static shot of the engine mounted on a test stand in a sunlit, open field, as the powerful engine roar and music persist. Meagan Roth, Sr. Manager Launch and Test Operations at Firefly Aerospace, wearing a black Firefly-branded polo shirt with sunglasses, stands beside the engine and begins to speak with a clear, authoritative voice, stating, "This is the Reaver engine. This is our first stage engine. This engine is essentially what will be installed on the vehicle." Inside a test facility, two engineers are seen working under the engine, inspecting components, while Meagan continues, "Every engine that we will fly, go through what's called an acceptance testing protocol." The scene shifts to a nighttime test with a greenish glow from the engine’s exhaust, as Meagan elaborates on the ATP's purpose: "And what ATP is seeking to do is both tune the engine, so make sure we have the right amount of propellants in the right combination, as well as ensure the workmanship and quality of that engine itself." The video then shows the engine inside a test chamber, with the background music continuing. Meagan walks up a metal staircase to an observation deck, and from the deck, she gestures toward the launch site, explaining, "We are standing on TSQ, which is the stand that we use for testing the various rocket stages, so stage one and stage two, both get tested right here." A wide aerial view shows the rocket on the pad, as Meagan adds, "That actually is where the fire goes through when we ignite the engine." The segment concludes with a dramatic aerial shot of the rocket igniting, producing a massive plume of fire and smoke as it lifts off from the launch pad, with the surrounding landscape visible in the background, accompanied by a powerful return of the engine roar and swelling dramatic music. The CNBC logo is consistently displayed in the top right corner throughout the segment.

event_blocks.summary: At a launch site, a woman in a black Firefly-branded polo shirt, with a rocket in the background, stated, "condition so that when we get there, it's game day," as light, hopeful music began. The scene shifted to a drive through a rural area, passing a 'YOU’RE IN SPACE COUNTRY...' sign, then showing industrial infrastructure and a large white pipeline at Vandenberg Space Force Base in California. An aerial view revealed a long white rocket being transported on a truck along a rural road. In a control room, monitors displayed live feeds of a rocket on the launch pad, preceding a dramatic launch sequence with flames and smoke. Inside a workshop, Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, explained the rigorous testing and integration process: "Once things are rigorously tested in Texas, and we transport them to the launch sites, do the final integration of the vehicle, put them out on the launch pad, and we just do one final hot fire test where we put the rocket up, we pretend we're going to launch it, we light it, we let it run and just verify that everything's in perfect order before we actually just do the final thing, which is let it fly." This narration coincided with footage of the rocket on the launch pad, including a close-up of its engine firing, amidst an exchange about "16% on O2?" and "Made it all the way," accompanied by the sounds of a rocket engine firing and a whoosh. A sign for 'FIREFLY AEROSPACE SPACE LAUNCH COMPLEX 2W' was displayed as workers in hard hats inspected the rocket’s base, with one man lying on his back to examine the engines. On the launch site, a man in a black Firefly hoodie remarked, "We've been working on this for years and years. A lot of money's gone into this and tomorrow's the the payoff," and a woman in a green shirt and yellow hard hat added, "It's just such an incredible feeling to finally be at the..." as the music faded, with the CNBC logo visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: Photographers and spectators were silhouetted against a clear sky as a rocket ascended with a distinct whooshing sound, but the tall, black and white Firefly rocket dramatically exploded mid-flight, accompanied by a loud blast and an exclamation of 'Oh!'. In the 'FIREFLY AEROSPACE' control room, engineers in headsets and masks reacted with concern while monitoring screens, as a calm male voice reported, 'Anomaly team is confirmed' and specified, 'Anomaly team is here MLT 1499.' Tom Markusic, Firefly Aerospace's Founder and CEO, appeared in a workshop with rocket components, calmly explaining that the first flight failure occurred when 'one of the engines simply just turned off in flight,' attributing it to 'just a simple electrical connector that that rattled loose.' Markusic reflected on the immense effort and how failure 'feels like a lot of other things that happen in this company,' including financial struggles and layoffs, before 'booting back up,' describing the experience as 'deeply troubling' but emphasizing the 'conviction that ultimately you'll be successful.' Michael Sheetz, in a dark suit, spoke from a well-lit living room, noting that 'a lot of companies over the year have attempted the same thing and failed to do so on their first tries, but have been ultimately successful,' concluding that this is 'a business that is quite unforgiving.' The segment concluded with a close-up of the Firefly rocket on the launch pad during ignition, with intense flames and smoke erupting from its base, before the explosion sequence repeated, all under the visible CNBC logo.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with an infographic comparing space companies' costs and payload capacities for low-Earth orbit missions, highlighting Firefly Aerospace's $15M per launch for up to 1,000 kg, contrasted with SpaceX's Falcon 9 at approximately $62M per launch. Graphics showcase Firefly's rocket lineup, including the sleek blue Firefly Alpha and the larger Firefly Beta, with an expected capacity of up to 8,000 kg, followed by a technical diagram and a conceptual rendering of the futuristic, reusable Firefly Gamma rocket plane. Michael Sheetz, in a dark suit, states that strategic partnerships will enable hardware flights within a couple of years, while an engineer works on a 3D spacecraft model on dual monitors, and a female voice mentions Firefly's plans for a winged reusable rocket. Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, seated in a workshop amidst large rocket components, explains that long-term development projects await the establishment of core businesses, clarifying that Firefly is an 'end-to-end space transportation company' where rockets, representing one-third of their architecture, are essential but not the sole focus. The scene shifts to a model of the Blue Ghost lunar lander in a cleanroom, complete with solar panels and landing legs, as eerie, atmospheric music plays. A graphic of the Blue Ghost lander on the Moon details commercially available resources for a 2023 mission, with Markusic describing it as their lunar lander solution. Later, Markusic explains the Blue Ghost lander prototype, a black and gold structure with extended solar panels and landing legs in a workshop, fulfills NASA's commercial lunar payload services mission, having won a $93 million contract for 10 payloads under 100 kilograms. Markusic, wearing a 'Blue Ghost' t-shirt, is seen speaking to the camera as a female narrator notes NASA's Artemis program aims to return humans to the Moon, with projects like Blue Ghost delivering scientific research. A NASA animation depicts a rocket on a launch pad and astronauts on the lunar surface with a lander and rover. Michael Sheetz discusses how companies like Blue Origin and SpaceX are developing human landers, and these precursor missions are smaller science missions to better understand the lunar environment, noting NASA's shift towards being a customer rather than a manufacturer, with awards in the $70 to $100 million range. Sheetz, in a well-lit living room, speaks about this program, followed by shots of engineers assembling spacecraft in a NASA hangar and close-ups of the lander's components. Bethany Baldwin-Pulcini, Systems Engineer at Firefly Aerospace, describes payloads studying lunar regolith, performing science experiments, and analyzing the magnetic field. The CNBC logo is visible throughout.

event_blocks.summary: In a workshop, a woman in a dark zip-up top discusses the future importance of orbital services, as dramatic rocket launches, both day and night, fill the screen with intense flames and smoke, signaling an increase in launches with 114 orbital launches in 2020, 41 commercial. Chad Anderson, in a white shirt, explains the transition into an 'entrepreneurial space age' and a coming 'SpaceX age' with Starship, projecting a drastic cost reduction per kilogram from $100,000 in the shuttle era to $1,000-ish with Falcon Heavy, and eventually $10 with Starship, while visuals show a SpaceX rocket on a launch pad and a fiery shuttle launch. raw_segments: The video opens with a woman in a dark zip-up top speaking animatedly in a workshop, a large rocket section visible behind her, as she discusses how objects that 'can just hang out in orbit and transport things, that can service things, that can relay things' will be 'really big for us'. Upbeat background music swells as the scene transitions to dramatic nighttime and daytime rocket launches, featuring intense flames and smoke. A narrator states that 'launches are picking up', with 114 orbital launches in 2020, 41 of which were commercial. Next, Chad Anderson, a man in a white shirt, appears in a modern office with a space helmet on a stand behind him, explaining that we are in an 'entrepreneurial space age' moving into a 'SpaceX age' when Starship comes online, dramatically reducing costs from '$100,000 per kilogram' in the shuttle era to '$1,000-ish dollars a kilogram' with the Falcon Heavy, and ultimately to '$10 a kilogram' with Starship. The visuals then show a group observing a SpaceX rocket on a launch pad, a close-up of a rocket under the sun, and a fiery space shuttle launch. Engineers in blue suits are seen working on a satellite in a cleanroom with Korean text. A CNBC article headline appears, stating 'Bank of America expects the space industry to triple to a $1.4 trillion market within a decade', with the narrator confirming the global space economy is already worth over $423 billion and is expected to reach $1.4 trillion by 2030. Finally, Tom Markusic, in a black t-shirt, stands in a workshop with a large rocket component, stating that 'Space is the next frontier for the information revolution' following the growth of the internet, as an animated visualization of Earth surrounded by a dense network of orbiting satellites, illustrating global connectivity, zooms out.

The video opens with a dynamic animation of Earth surrounded by a dense network of orbiting satellites and space debris, emphasizing the crowded low-Earth orbit. A male voice, identified as Michael Sheetz, explains how services will be served from space, enabling more efficient information distribution and providing 'internet from the sky, internet everywhere.' The scene transitions to a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket on a launch pad, followed by a satellite deploying its solar panels in orbit above a swirling storm system. Chad Anderson, speaking from a modern office with a space helmet behind him, elaborates that the billions spent in the past decade have primarily centered on building rockets or satellites, with steady growth in subsectors like suborbital space tourism, in-space manufacturing, and other data services. Michael Sheetz reappears, noting that companies are starting to go public or get acquired. A female voice then highlights high investor interest in SPACs, mentioning that both Virgin Orbit, shown as a white spaceplane carried under a carrier aircraft, and Rocket Lab, with its Electron rocket being assembled and then on a launch pad, have leveraged this to raise additional capital. Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, seen in a workshop with large rocket sections, discusses going public as a way to fund development, suggesting a public offering for Firefly might be possible next year. The female voice concludes, stating that the company faces significant competition, with several startups aggressively fundraising and working to get rockets off the ground, as an aerial view of a launch site and a close-up of Astra’s small, silver rocket being assembled are shown.

The segment begins with a time-lapse exterior shot of a SpaceX facility at dusk, showing a tall white rocket illuminated by streetlights as cars create light trails. This transitions to an indoor interview where Chad Anderson, in a white shirt, speaks about tracking 'a little less than 100 companies that have been started by SpaceX alumni.' Next, Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, appears in a workshop, stating in his voice that 'there are very few elite crowd that can actually do it' and that they will 'enter that elite stratosphere' with their second rocket. A dramatic rocket launch of a black and white Firefly rocket ignites and ascends, followed by control room engineers monitoring screens, while a female narrator's voice explains that 'once Firefly and others succeed in reaching space, they will be confronted with another challenge: building and refining operations for regular spaceflight.' The scene shifts to a workshop with large black rocket sections, where Lauren Lyons, a woman with curly hair, declares in her voice, 'We want to launch 24 rockets in 2024,' emphasizing the need to 'fly our next flight as soon as possible' to 'conquer that learning curve' and 'step into repeatable builds.' A 'The Verge' title card then announces a secret rocket engine IP deal between Astra and Firefly, as Chad Anderson's voice returns, stating that 'for them to be a viable business, they're going to need to get into other things.' Finally, Michael Sheetz, in a dark suit, speaks directly to the camera, with the female narrator's voice mentioning 'the company is reportedly in talks to provide engine technology to competing launch company Astra,' before Sheetz's voice concludes, 'The contract specified as many as 50 of the Reaver engines for Astra to use on their own.' The CNBC logo remains visible throughout.

The video opens with Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, seated on a stool in a workshop, wearing a black 'FIREFLY' polo shirt, speaking directly to the camera about helping other companies and making money by sharing their rocket technology and future developments. A female narrator then explains that Firefly sees selling components as a big business, as visuals show a 3D model of a rocket engine and engineers working on a rocket component. As a large rocket is lifted by a yellow crane at a launch site, Tom Markusic elaborates on their vision for an e-commerce marketplace where customers can anonymously buy rocket components like engines and valves, similar to Amazon. He then appears in a different workshop, now in a black 'Blue Ghost' t-shirt with a lunar lander prototype behind him, using animated gestures to describe a future where Firefly components will be on most missions. The female narrator acknowledges the industry's inherent risks and tough road ahead, as the scene shifts to a control room where engineers, wearing headsets and masks, monitor screens. A male voice discusses the cyclical nature of rocket companies, experiencing both very low lows and very high highs. The segment concludes with the Firefly rocket fully assembled on the launch pad under a clear blue sky, as Tom Markusic predicts industry consolidation, stating there isn't room for many launcher companies. The CNBC logo remains consistently in the top right corner.

The video opens with a close-up of a white rocket nose cone, featuring a black stylized logo and an American flag against a clear blue sky, as Tom Markusic, Founder and CEO of Firefly Aerospace, speaks about teaming up and forming new enterprises with viable players for the future, accompanied by a light, hopeful synth background music. The scene transitions to Markusic, in a black 'Blue Ghost' t-shirt, speaking in a workshop with a large lander prototype behind him. Following this, Michael Sheetz, in a dark suit and light blue shirt, appears in a well-lit living room with large windows, speaking directly to the camera about companies being acquired and seeding industry growth, while the hopeful synth music continues. A dramatic rocket launch then shows a white rocket igniting and ascending from a launch pad with tall towers and palm trees, as a female narrator emphasizes that a future in the stars isn't far off, accompanied by more dramatic, orchestral music. This is followed by a stunning view of Earth from space with a rising sun, then archival footage of an Apollo lunar module on the Moon where an astronaut plants an American flag, underscored by Neil Armstrong's iconic "That's one small step..." quote. Tom Markusic's voice returns, expressing a desire to avoid another "bubble" like the one after the initial Moon landing in the late 60s/early 70s, where technology was forgotten. A NASA animation then depicts a lander descending to the Moon’s surface, followed by astronauts on Mars near a habitat, as Markusic continues to speak about laying the groundwork for future generations to reach the Moon, Mars, and beyond, with the music building in intensity. The video shifts to a black rocket labeled 'FIREFLY' on a launch pad under a clear blue sky, with a man in a black jacket and sunglasses walking nearby. A man in a black hoodie is also seen walking toward the rocket. Markusic's voice reflects on the difficulty of the endeavor but expresses his continued passion and feeling of being blessed. The final scenes show a group of people in a control room celebrating with a cake, and a man in a black jacket inspecting the rocket’s base, lying on his back to examine the engines, all set to emotional and inspiring music. The CNBC logo is consistently visible in the top right corner throughout the segment.

The segment opens with engineers and technicians gathered around a large, complex rocket engine on a concrete pad, as a man in a black jacket and sunglasses gestures towards it while another in a blue sweater listens. The scene then shifts to a close-up of a man in a black jacket with a yellow Firefly logo on the back, standing with others, including a woman in a mask and a man in a yellow hard hat, all observing the rocket. A tall black rocket labeled 'FIREFLY' is then shown on a launch pad, silhouetted against a bright sky, followed by a control room where engineers, including Control Room Engineers, monitor multiple screens displaying live launch data and a large projection of the rocket. The FIREFLY Rocket ignites with a burst of flame and smoke, launching into the sky, leading to a celebratory room where a crowd cheers and raises their hands, watching the launch on a large screen. A Man in Firefly Shirt is then shown speaking in a workshop with a large rocket component behind him, passionately explaining how their work is 'creating great 21st-century jobs' and how people recognize the Firefly logo, telling him, 'Hey, I was with you the whole time. I was watching that launch and everything. It was so exciting.' He emphasizes that what they are doing is 'not only inspiring to me... but it's inspiring to to thousands of people,' concluding, 'I can't get enough of this, and I hope I get to keep doing it for a long, long time.' Soft, uplifting background music begins during his speech and continues as the video transitions to an aerial view of the launch site, showing the rocket on the pad with support vehicles. The segment concludes with CNBC credits, listing producers, graphics, and Additional Footage Credits from sources like Firefly Aerospace, NASA, and SpaceX, with the CNBC Logo visible throughout, followed by a call to subscribe and comment, all set to the continuing inspirational music.

global_entity_library.Rocket Engine Close-up: A detailed view of a Firefly rocket engine with a carbon fiber nozzle, yellow gear, and metallic piping, with the 'FIREFLY' label visible on the nozzle, shown as Tom Markusic discusses the high 'burn rates' in the space business.
global_entity_library.Launch Site: An arid, open landscape with a launch pad, support structures, and a distant tower, marked by the on-screen text 'September 2, 2021' and 'First test launch'.
global_entity_library.Man in Black Jacket: A man wearing a black jacket and sunglasses, gesturing toward a rocket engine on a launch pad, later seen from behind with a yellow Firefly logo on his jacket.
global_entity_library.Man in Black Hoodie: A man wearing a black hoodie, walking near the rocket's base during inspection, with the rocket and launch pad visible in the background.
global_entity_library.Shea Ferring: SVP of Spacecraft at Firefly Aerospace, seen wearing a black Firefly-branded shirt and speaking in a workshop with a golden spacecraft in the background, he explains that Firefly 'build[s] everything ourselves' to control cost and schedule, detailing a four-year development timeline including 'a year of design,' 'two years of developmental testing,' and 'one year of qualifying hardware,' further emphasizing the need for 'super high propulsion, super light structures' that are 'very, very efficient' but 'hard to test'.
global_entity_library.Rocket Engine: A large, complex rocket engine, identified by Meagan Roth as the 'Reaver engine' and Firefly's first-stage engine, with visible piping, valves, and a metallic nozzle, shown during multiple test firings with intense flames and smoke, producing a powerful rumbling roar, mounted on a test stand in an open field and inside a test chamber. Its purpose is to be installed on the vehicle, and it undergoes an 'acceptance testing protocol' (ATP) to tune it and ensure workmanship and quality.
global_entity_library.Meagan Roth: Sr. Manager Launch and Test Operations at Firefly Aerospace, wearing a black Firefly-branded polo shirt with a white logo and sunglasses, speaking to the camera beside a rocket engine and later on an observation deck, with a clear, authoritative voice, explaining the purpose and testing of the 'Reaver engine' and the 'TSQ' test stand.
global_entity_library.Test Facility: An industrial test site with metal structures, support towers, and a concrete pad, identified by Meagan Roth as 'TSQ' (Test Stand Quad), located in a flat, grassy landscape under a clear sky, featuring a large engine test stand for testing rocket stages one and two, and an observation deck. It is where the engine's fire goes through during ignition.
global_entity_library.Woman in Green Shirt: A woman with curly hair, wearing a green zip-up top and a yellow hard hat, speaking to the camera on the launch site with the rocket in the background, who expresses "such an incredible feeling" about finally being there.
global_entity_library.Vandenberg Space Force Base, California: A remote, arid landscape with industrial buildings, white pipelines, and satellite dishes, identified by on-screen text as the location.

global_entity_library.Woman in Firefly Shirt: A woman with long brown hair, wearing a black Firefly-branded polo shirt with an American flag patch on the sleeve, standing on an observation deck with a rocket in the background, who states, "condition so that when we get there, it's game day."

global_entity_library.Bethany Baldwin-Pulcini: A woman with long dark hair and glasses, wearing a gray cardigan over a black top, actively pointing and explaining components of the Blue Ghost lander in a workshop setting, speaking clearly and informatively about its retroreflector and the LMS payload.
global_entity_library.Ray Allensworth: Deputy Program Manager at Firefly Aerospace, wearing a black long-sleeve shirt, seen seated in a workshop with a golden spacecraft and lander components in the background. She speaks clearly about recreating the lunar surface for simulations and using the 'lunar lander sim' for their vision navigation system to 'eliminate as many unknowns as possible'.
global_entity_library.Firefly’s Space Utility Vehicle (SUV): A digital presentation slide showing the SUV’s design, capabilities, and potential applications, including deploying satellites and providing in-space services.
